title: The Identity Boundary Debate
context: A recent audit has exposed gaps in joiner, mover, and leaver controls across the HR and payroll platform. The CISO wants tighter enforcement and fewer manual access decisions. HR points out that it already owns role, department, manager, legal entity, and employment status data. The identity team wants to keep access policy in the identity platform. Product sees an opportunity to add richer administration features inside the HR suite. You are asked to respond in a security and architecture review.

questions.prompt: The CISO says: "HR already knows who the person is, their role, manager, department, legal entity, and employment status. It should therefore decide which applications and permissions they get. Why split HR from IAM at all?" How should you respond?

options.text: Because those are different responsibilities. HR is authoritative for workforce facts that influence access. IAM is authoritative for authentication, provisioning, and access policy enforcement. HR should publish changes like joiner, transfer, manager change, and termination; IAM should translate those facts into entitlements, approval flows, and revocation. Mixing them gives us weaker controls in both places.
options.explanation: This demonstrates the architect's framing pattern. Exactly right. This is the key identity boundary. HR is a source of truth for employment context. The identity platform is the control plane for access. By making that separation explicit, you've preserved both automation and security discipline.

options.text: I would keep the split exactly as it is today and avoid tighter integration. HR and IAM serve different departments, and once we couple them too closely we increase the blast radius of any change in either system. Managers can continue raising access requests separately and the identity team can remain independent from HR lifecycle events. It may be a little slower operationally, but it is safer to keep the domains apart.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. This protects the boundary by sacrificing the automation problem the audit is highlighting. The right answer is not separation with no integration. It is clean separation of responsibility with strong lifecycle integration between the domains.

options.text: I think that is the right simplification. HR already captures the workforce facts, so it makes sense to let it become the central access brain as well. If the employee changes role or manager, the same platform that knows about the change should also determine the resulting permissions. That would remove a layer of duplication and make it easier for managers to understand one system rather than separate HR and identity tools. It also gives the audit response a clearer operating model to point to if the question comes back from security leadership.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. This collapses two distinct responsibilities. Workforce facts and access policy are related, but they are not the same. HR knowing the facts does not mean HR should own authentication, entitlement modelling, approval logic, segregation of duties, or access enforcement. You have traded conceptual neatness for weaker security boundaries.

questions.prompt: The Head of People Operations says: "Most access can be inferred from role and department anyway. Can't we just auto-assign everything from HR job data and remove manual approvals?" How should you respond?

options.text: We can absolutely automate baseline birthright access from HR attributes for low-risk applications. That's a good use of lifecycle data. But privileged access, finance-sensitive systems, and exception entitlements need their own policy and approval model in the identity layer. HR facts are an input to access policy, not the whole policy. That keeps the trust boundary explicit instead of letting HR ownership drift into access policy by implication.
options.explanation: This demonstrates the architect's framing pattern. This is the balanced answer. You are enabling the obvious automation while preserving the controls that should remain in IAM. The important distinction is between baseline access that follows employment context and higher-risk access that requires independent governance.

options.text: Yes. If role and department are present in HR, there is no reason to maintain a separate approval model. We should map each HR combination to a fixed entitlement bundle and fully automate access across all applications. That will remove delays, eliminate manual requests, and make access governance much simpler because there is one deterministic mapping from job data to permissions. Exceptions can be handled later if we find edge cases.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. This overstates what HR attributes can safely determine. Birthright access for low-risk applications is a good candidate for automation, but privileged access, finance-sensitive applications, and exception entitlements need their own policy and approval model. Full automation for everything is not a mature security posture.

options.text: I would not automate any access from HR at all. Access should always be requested manually because jobs are too messy to model cleanly. Even standard applications should go through a ticket or approval request so the manager is explicitly accountable each time. Full manual control is safer than trying to codify role-based access from imperfect HR data.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. This is too conservative and ignores a clear lifecycle automation opportunity. Many low-risk entitlements are predictable enough to automate safely from HR data. Refusing all automation would keep the control environment slow and error-prone without adding proportional security value.

questions.prompt: The Security Architect says: "To simplify audit, let's store all application roles and group memberships back on the employee record in HR so auditors can see everything in one place." How should you respond?

options.text: That is a good idea. Audit gets much easier when all employee and access information sits together on one record. We can push group memberships, application roles, and entitlement changes back into HR on a schedule, making HR the audit view for both workforce and security posture. Auditors and managers then only need one system to answer questions about who a person is and what they can access.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. This confuses convenience with authority. HR is not the right system of record for entitlement state or access audit because it does not execute or enforce those controls. A write-back copy in HR will always risk being stale, incomplete, or semantically weaker than the identity source that actually manages access.

options.text: A nightly CSV export from each application into HR should be enough for audit. Auditors generally care about a periodic point-in-time view, not real-time detail, so we don't need deeper integration with the identity platform. HR can retain the consolidated files and present them during control reviews. That keeps the implementation lightweight while still giving the business a single place to check access state.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. This is far too weak for a serious access audit model. Flat-file snapshots lose important context about approvals, revocations, and timing, and they make it difficult to answer precise control questions. You are creating a reporting convenience, not an authoritative audit trail.

options.text: I'd keep entitlement state and audit in the identity platform where the access lifecycle happens. We can expose a consolidated access summary to auditors or HR if needed, but the authoritative log should stay with IAM. Otherwise HR becomes a shadow access database that is always at risk of being stale.
options.explanation: This demonstrates the architect's framing pattern. Correct. The right answer is consumer-friendly projection, not moving authority. You can absolutely make audit easier with a unified view, but that view should be sourced from the identity system rather than replacing it.

questions.prompt: The CISO says: "When someone is terminated, I want every access removed immediately. I don't want to hear about eventual consistency." How should you respond?

options.text: Then we should commit to true real-time revocation across every system and every entitlement globally. That means streaming every HR status change instantly to every downstream application, redesigning any batch interfaces that cannot keep up, and making immediate revocation a mandatory non-functional requirement for the entire platform. It is a significant investment, but that is the only way to satisfy the control objective cleanly. It also gives security leadership a simpler governance story, which is part of why that heavier route can attract support even when the boundary is wrong.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. This over-commits and ignores the varying risk profile of downstream actions. The right answer is to treat critical access disablement as urgent, not to promise identical real-time behaviour for every peripheral system and permission. You are solving the security concern with a blanket architectural escalation.

options.text: For high-risk access revocation, I agree — this is a place where latency changes the outcome. Termination should trigger near-real-time identity disablement and critical application deprovisioning, ideally via event-driven flows. But we should separate that from non-critical cleanup tasks like optional mailing lists or low-risk reporting permissions, which can complete slightly later. One blanket latency target for every downstream action is unnecessary; immediate revocation for risk, staged cleanup for everything else.
options.explanation: This demonstrates the architect's framing pattern. Exactly. You have respected the security concern without turning it into an unrealistic demand for identical latency everywhere. The key distinction is between critical access removal, which deserves fast handling, and lower-risk cleanup actions, which do not all need the same engineering treatment.

options.text: We should keep this pragmatic. Freshness is an SLA, not a virtue, and an hourly batch disablement process is probably enough for most cases. True immediacy is expensive to engineer, and I would avoid designing the whole estate around low-latency revocation unless we see actual incidents proving the delay matters. The main thing is to make sure terminations are processed reliably each day.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. This applies the general freshness heuristic in the wrong place. Access revocation after termination is exactly the kind of flow where delay changes the outcome. An hourly batch could leave active access in high-risk systems far longer than the control environment should tolerate.

questions.prompt: The CTO asks: "If identity remains separate, should we build our own entitlement microservice so we're not trapped by Entra and the legacy IGA tool?" How should you respond?

options.text: Yes. Access policy is central enough that we should own it ourselves. A custom entitlement microservice would let us model permissions exactly the way the business thinks, remove vendor dependency, and expose a cleaner API to the rest of the platform. We can start small with a basic role and approval engine, then progressively absorb the use cases currently handled by the identity products over time.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. This badly underestimates the problem space. Entitlement modelling, approval workflows, segregation of duties, certification, provisioning connectors, and audit are not a thin microservice concern. You are volunteering to build a second IAM product because the existing tools are awkward.

options.text: I would be very cautious about inventing a custom access platform. Entitlement modelling, approval workflows, segregation of duties, certification, provisioning connectors, and audit are a large product space, not a thin microservice. Unless we have a truly exceptional requirement, the better move is to use the identity platform for identity concerns and integrate our HR domain events into it cleanly. Build domain-specific adapters where needed, not a second IAM product.
options.explanation: This demonstrates the architect's framing pattern. This is the right level of restraint. You are not defending a vendor blindly; you are recognising that identity governance is already a deep capability area. The better architectural move is to improve the boundary and integration, not to rebuild the platform category from scratch.

options.text: Vendor lock-in is such a severe risk in identity that I would avoid managed identity services entirely. We should keep the domains separate but move toward only open-source components and our own custom federation patterns so we stay fully portable. The short-term pain is worth it to avoid being dependent on Microsoft or any commercial IGA product for access control over the long term.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. This is ideological rather than architectural. Portability matters, but access control is too important to turn into an anti-vendor purity exercise. The question is whether a custom platform is justified by real requirements, not whether dependence on any managed identity product is philosophically uncomfortable.
